metadata.title: Strong and Weak Technologies Knowledge Graph
metadata.description: Concept dependency graph covering strong vs weak technology pairs, metaverse ingredients, Web3 GTM strategies, and scissor labels — 18 nodes, 20 edges
metadata.source: Chris Dixon 'Strong and Weak Technologies' (2019), a16z crypto 'Metaverse Ingredients' (2022), Maggie Hsu 'Web3 GTM' (2022), John Palmer 'Scissor Labels' (2021)

nodes.name: Strong vs Weak Technologies
nodes.description: Chris Dixon's framework distinguishing technology pairs where strong forms reshape the world while weak forms adapt to it. Strong tech captivates enthusiasts and follows hype cycles, while weak tech appears safer to incumbents.
key_ideas: Technologies emerge in pairs: strong (revolutionary) and weak (compromise)
Strong tech: iPhone, public internet, permissionless blockchain, VR
Weak tech: Blackberry, intranets, private blockchain, AR
Strong tech follows Perez/Gartner hype cycle through trough of disillusionment

nodes.name: Hype Cycle
nodes.description: Perez/Gartner model describing how strong technologies progress: innovation trigger, peak of inflated expectations, trough of disillusionment, slope of enlightenment, plateau of productivity.
key_ideas: Five phases: trigger → peak → trough → slope → plateau
Trough of disillusionment is when entrepreneurs lose faith and pivot to weak tech
Strong technologies that survive the trough define new eras
Weak tech appears closer to mainstream adoption but rarely defines the future

nodes.name: The Unreasonable Man
nodes.description: George Bernard Shaw's concept that progress depends on those who persist in adapting the world to their vision. Weak tech adapts to the current world; strong tech makes the world adapt to it.
key_ideas: Reasonable people adapt themselves to the world
Unreasonable people adapt the world to themselves
All progress depends on the unreasonable
iPhone made the world change (carriers, developers, users) rather than conforming

nodes.name: Decentralization as Essential Ingredient
nodes.description: The dominant and fundamental principle of a proper metaverse. Systems not owned or operated by a single entity or oligarchy, enabled by blockchain's code-enforced commitments.
key_ideas: Centralized platforms start friendly, turn extractive as growth slows
Without decentralization, users can be 'rugged' at any time
Blockchain enables strong commitments through code vs weak promises
Decentralization prevents single-entity capture and abuse
nodes.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)

nodes.name: Property Rights in Digital Worlds
nodes.description: The principle that digital asset purchases should grant true ownership like physical world, not rentals. Enabled by cryptography, blockchain, and NFTs to convert digital serfs into homesteaders.
key_ideas: Current in-game purchases are rentals, not ownership
True ownership means ability to sell, trade, and port across contexts
NFTs and blockchain make digital property rights enforceable
Metaverse should convert digital serfs into homesteaders
nodes.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)

nodes.name: Self-Sovereign Identity
nodes.description: Identity that persists across metaverse without dependence on centralized providers. Web3 cryptography enables authentication without surveillance-based social login.
key_ideas: Cannot own property if you don't own yourself
Current SSO (Google, Meta) enables tracking for ad models
Wallets (MetaMask, Phantom) provide self-verification
Standards like EIP-4361 and ENS enable open identity
nodes.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)

nodes.name: Composability
nodes.description: Ability to combine software components like Lego blocks. Each component written once, reused forever. Strong form enables permissionless modification across entire stack, as demonstrated by DeFi.
key_ideas: Lego-like combination of software components
Strong form: permissionless modification at all layers (DeFi)
Weak form: blackbox APIs you can't modify (Stripe, Twilio)
Unlocks exponential value like compound interest or Moore's Law
nodes.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)

nodes.name: Openness and Open Source
nodes.description: Transparent code, algorithms, and protocols as public goods. Essential for true composability and community trust. Enables safer software and reduces information asymmetry.
key_ideas: Open source enables true composability
Transparency makes software safer and more trustworthy
Economic conditions become clear to all parties
Removes information asymmetry between builders and users
nodes.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)

nodes.name: Community Ownership
nodes.description: Stakeholders have governance voice proportional to participation, not corporate edicts. Tokens align network participants toward common good. DAOs experiment with flexible governance.
key_ideas: Stakeholders govern proportional to contribution
Tokens align builders, creators, investors, users
DAOs enable flexible, democratic governance experiments
Prevents single entity from owning/controlling virtual worlds
nodes.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)

nodes.name: Social Immersion
nodes.description: Metaverse requires social immersion, not necessarily VR/AR hardware. The Trojan horse narrative from Big Tech. What matters is enabled activities: hangouts, work, socializing.
key_ideas: VR/AR hardware not essential for metaverse
Big Tech uses hardware requirement as Trojan horse for control
Social immersion can happen via Discord, Twitter Spaces, Clubhouse
Activities matter more than hardware: socializing, working, earning
nodes.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)

nodes.name: Cold Start Problem
nodes.description: How to bootstrap a network from nothing. Web2 uses traditional sales/marketing funnels. Web3 uses tokens to reward early users before network effects kick in.
key_ideas: Every business faces: how to get first customers?
Web2: invest in sales/marketing teams for lead generation
Web3: tokens reward early users for pre-network-effect contributions
Early users become evangelists more powerful than salespeople

nodes.name: Tokens as GTM Catalyst
nodes.description: Tokens provide alternative to cold start problem by rewarding early contributions. Compound's COMP liquidity mining drove TVL from $100M to $600M. Tokens attract but don't guarantee retention.
key_ideas: Tokens reward early users before network effects emerge
Compound liquidity mining: $100M to $600M TVL
Early users become evangelists and network builders
Caveat: token incentives alone insufficient for stickiness

nodes.name: Web3 GTM Stakeholders
nodes.description: Web3 GTM targets customers, developers, investors, and partners — not just customers. Community roles more important than sales/marketing roles. Focus on purpose and community ownership.
key_ideas: Web2 GTM: customers (via sales/marketing)
Web3 GTM: customers + developers + investors + partners
Community > sales/marketing in importance
Product not necessarily first; purpose and community can precede

nodes.name: DeFi DAO GTM
nodes.description: DeFi protocols start centralized, progressively decentralize via governance tokens and DAO. Value accrues differently than Web2. TVL is canonical metric but integrations and community health matter more.
key_ideas: Path: centralized dev team → governance token → DAO ownership
MakerDAO (2015 DAO, 2018 Foundation, 2021 Foundation retirement)
Uniswap: UNI holders control, Uniswap Labs just one contributor
Better metrics: token holders, community engagement, integrations > TVL

nodes.name: Social and Cultural DAO GTM
nodes.description: GTM centers on clear purpose and organic community growth. ConstitutionDAO raised $47M without roadmap, execution plan, or token. Purpose can precede method. Metrics include Discord activity and governance participation.
key_ideas: Start with purpose, method follows (vs Web2 crowdfunding)
ConstitutionDAO: $47M in weeks, no roadmap or token initially
Krause House (NBA team), LinksDAO (golf), PleasrDAO (NFTs)
Metrics: Discord activity, retention, governance votes, paid contributors

nodes.name: Scissor Labels
nodes.description: Categorization terms that gain widespread recognition as trend names but lack agreed definitions. Controlling the definition means controlling the trend narrative. Examples: Hyperpop, Metaverse, Web3.
key_ideas: Establishes trend name widely but definition remains contested
Controlling definition = controlling what the trend represents
Disguises power struggles as intellectual debates
Examples: Hyperpop, Spatial Software, Metaverse, Web3

nodes.name: Narrative Wars
nodes.description: Scissor labels become battlegrounds for narrative control. In VC-funded spaces, being inside the label definition affects funding. Binary debates obscure real stakes: community preservation, cultural capital.
key_ideas: Scissor label definition battles are power struggles
VC narrative inclusion affects startup funding
Hyperpop debate was about preserving queer/trans community space
Binary framing (is X included?) hides nuanced stakes

nodes.name: Future Orientation vs Scissor Debate
nodes.description: Productive engagement with scissor labels: identify real stakes, introduce spectrums/subcategories, or ignore and focus on the interesting idea underneath. Best approach: keep creating forward.
key_ideas: Move 1: Identify and surface actual stakes beyond binary question
Move 2: Break binary with spectrums or subcategories
Scissor labels emerge when something genuinely interesting appears
Best response: keep creating, influence category through work not debate

edges.description: Hype cycle is a characteristic pattern of strong technologies
Strong tech embodies Shaw's unreasonable man philosophy
Decentralization makes enforceable property rights possible
Decentralization prevents unilateral control, enables community governance
Cannot own property without owning your identity
Open source code is prerequisite for true composability
DeFi leverages composability to build on existing protocols
Social DAOs are built on community ownership principle
Permissionless blockchain (strong) vs private blockchain (weak)
VR (strong) vs AR (weak) debate in metaverse context
Tokens solve cold start by incentivizing early users
Tokens enable DeFi liquidity mining strategies
Tokens coordinate social DAO members
DeFi DAOs must serve multiple stakeholder groups
Social DAOs coordinate diverse stakeholder types
Scissor labels become battlegrounds for narrative control
Productive response to narrative wars requires future orientation
Technology pair framing can clarify scissor label debates
Trough of disillusionment often coincides with scissor label debates
Metaverse is a scissor label, decentralization provides clarity
